items.title: Trump signs executive order on post-quantum cryptography
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order directing federal agencies to accelerate a nationwide migration to post-quantum cryptography.
items.details: The order directs the Office of Management and Budget and the National Cyber Director to lead an accelerated migration to post-quantum cryptography. It also directs the Department of Commerce, the National Security Agency, and the Department of Homeland Security to issue guidance to agencies on the transition.

items.title: GAO reports federal workforce decline during 2025
items.summary: The Government Accountability Office reported that the federal workforce at major agencies declined by more than 11% from December 2024 to January 2026.
items.details: GAO reported that data from 22 major federal agencies showed nearly 378,000 employees separated during 2025, while about 127,000 were hired. The total workforce across these agencies declined by nearly 256,000 employees, with declines ranging from about 1% at DHS to over 45% at the Department of Education.

items.title: Federal Reserve holds interest rates steady
items.summary: The Federal Reserve maintained the federal funds rate target range at 3.5% to 3.75%, effective June 18.
items.details: The Federal Open Market Committee directed operations to maintain the federal funds rate in a target range of 3.5% to 3.75%. The Board of Governors voted unanimously to maintain the interest rate paid on reserve balances at 3.65%.

items.title: U.S. sanctions networks tied to Sudan's war
items.summary: The United States sanctioned eight individuals and entities it said are fueling the conflict in Sudan and called for a humanitarian truce.
items.details: The Treasury Department said the sanctions target networks supporting both the Sudanese Armed Forces and the Rapid Support Forces, accusing them of supplying weapons, explosives, and foreign fighters. The State Department also announced measures under the Chemical and Biological Weapons Control and Warfare Elimination Act, including a ban on Sudanese state-owned air carriers operating in the United States.

items.title: U.S. hosts second Pax Silica Summit
items.summary: The State Department concluded the second Pax Silica Summit, adding ten new signatories to a declaration on technology supply chains.
items.details: The two-day summit, held June 25-26 in Washington, added ten partners as signatories to the Pax Silica Declaration, bringing the total to 24. New signatories include Argentina, Chile, Costa Rica, El Salvador, the European Union, Germany, Greece, Kazakhstan, the Netherlands, and Panama. The summit also produced a Joint Statement on AI Opportunity.

items.title: Congress passes 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act
items.summary: The House and Senate passed a bipartisan housing affordability package and sent it to the president's desk.
items.details: The Senate passed the bill 85-5 and the House passed it 358-32. The legislation aims to increase housing supply by easing regulations, and includes provisions limiting institutional investors' purchases of single-family homes. President Trump canceled a signing ceremony, saying he would not sign the bill until Congress passes the SAVE America Act.

items.title: House passes foreign policy and minerals bills
items.summary: The House passed bills including the U.S.-Japan-ROK Trilateral Cooperation Act and the DOMINANCE Act on critical energy minerals.
items.details: The House passed the bipartisan U.S.-Japan-ROK Trilateral Cooperation Act and H.R. 7037, the DOMINANCE Act, addressing overseas mineral investments and allied networks for critical energies. The bills were introduced by Representatives Ami Bera and Young Kim.
items.source: Office of U.S. Representative Ami Bera

items.title: Supreme Court allows end of TPS for Haiti and Syria
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that the administration can end Temporary Protected Status designations for Haiti and Syria.
items.details: In Mullin v. Doe, the court paused lower-court rulings that had barred ending the TPS designations, finding that federal law generally bars judicial review of TPS termination decisions. Justice Samuel Alito wrote for the majority; the court divided 6-3.

items.title: Supreme Court clears way to revive asylum 'metering' policy
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that the administration may revive a policy limiting daily asylum processing at the U.S.-Mexico border.
items.details: The justices overturned a lower-court order that had blocked the practice limiting the number of people who could apply for asylum each day. The majority agreed with the argument that migrants stopped before entering have not arrived in the country; Justice Sonia Sotomayor dissented from the bench.

items.title: Supreme Court strikes down state firearm permission laws
items.summary: In Wolford v. Lopez, the Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that states cannot require gun owners to get advance permission before bringing firearms onto private property.
items.details: The 6-3 decision held that requiring permission in advance is an undue burden on the right to possess and carry a firearm. The ruling affects laws in states including Hawaii, California, Maryland, New York, and New Jersey that required advance permission.

items.title: Tennessee court upholds new congressional map
items.summary: A three-judge panel in Davidson County dismissed a challenge to Tennessee's 2026 redistricting and election law changes, leaving the new map in place.
items.details: The panel ruled in favor of state officials, finding the legislature acted within the scope of the governor's special session call. The new map splits the Memphis-based 9th Congressional District, the state's only Democratic-held and majority-Black district. The Tennessee Democratic Party separately dropped its federal suit, and the NAACP said it would continue its challenge.

items.title: Supreme Court ruling reshapes 2026 redistricting landscape
items.summary: Following the Court's Louisiana v. Callais decision, multiple states have moved to redraw congressional maps ahead of the November midterms.
items.details: The 6-3 ruling narrowed Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act as applied to redistricting. Several states, including Texas, North Carolina, Missouri, Florida, California, and Tennessee, have new or revised congressional maps for 2026, while litigation continues in multiple states.

items.title: California holds gubernatorial primary
items.summary: California's top-two gubernatorial primary was held June 2, with Democrat Xavier Becerra and Republican Steve Hilton advancing to the November general election.
items.details: More than sixty candidates appeared on the June 2026 primary ballot to succeed term-limited Governor Gavin Newsom. The general election is scheduled for November 3, 2026.

OH.title: DeWine signs bills, vetoes mail-voting and submetering measures
OH.summary: Ohio Governor Mike DeWine signed several bills into law and issued vetoes, including of a mail-ballot bill and a utility submetering bill.
OH.details: DeWine signed bills addressing academic intervention services, memorial highways, and electric vehicle charging stations. He vetoed House Bill 472, a mail-voting measure, saying it was not needed, and vetoed House Bill 173 on utility submetering.
OH.source: Office of the Governor of Ohio

FL.title: DeSantis signs local government transparency and property tax bills
FL.summary: Florida Governor Ron DeSantis signed legislation on local government financial transparency and property tax relief.
FL.details: DeSantis signed Senate Bill 4-F and House Bill 1329, which require local governments to publish detailed financial information and conduct a budget reduction exercise before adopting a final budget. SB 4-F accompanies a proposed property tax constitutional amendment set for the November 2026 ballot.
FL.source: Executive Office of the Governor of Florida

AZ.title: Hobbs signs shooting-range protection bill
AZ.summary: Arizona Governor Katie Hobbs signed HB 2763, a bill addressing protections for a state shooting range.
AZ.details: The legislation, sponsored by Rep. Nguyen, passed the legislature with Republican support. The bill was signed into law in late June 2026.

CA.title: Newsom calls for national billionaires tax
CA.summary: California Governor Gavin Newsom said he supports a federal minimum tax on billionaires and closing certain tax loopholes.
CA.details: In a public post, Newsom said he supports a minimum tax on billionaires and ending what he called a 'tax-free lifestyle loan' tactic. He argued the issue should be addressed at the national rather than state level.

TN.title: Tennessee Democratic Party drops redistricting lawsuit
TN.summary: The Tennessee Democratic Party voluntarily dismissed its federal lawsuit challenging the state's new congressional map.
TN.details: A federal judge signed an order dismissing the case without prejudice, meaning it could be refiled. The party said it would shift focus to elections while continuing to support separate challenges filed by the NAACP and ACLU.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable events from cited sources without endorsement of any party, official, or position.
